title: Chapter 10: The First Ripple
content: The clinic opened on schedule.

Roxanne spent seven million Obols on medical supplies, equipment, and a protection contract that kept Nether-Grid entities away from the building. The Coalition sent three nurses and a doctor. Roxanne's Sanctuary contributed two nursing students and a surprisingly competent pre-med who had been hiding in the chemistry building.
For three days, things were almost normal.

People came to the campus. Word spread. Blackwood was safe — the only place in the city where you could sleep without a weapon in your hand. Roxanne set up a registration system: name, skills, needs. She didn't ask for payment. She didn't demand loyalty. She just... let people exist.
Lena documented everything. Her sketchbooks grew fat with drawings: the faces of survivors, the shapes of monsters, the strange violet sky that never changed.
Silas learned to cook.

That was the detail that broke Roxanne's brain the most. The four-billion-year-old god had discovered the campus kitchen and was working his way through every cookbook in the library. He made pasta from scratch. He baked bread. He refused to explain how an eldritch horror learned to make a perfect béchamel sauce.
"I observe," he said. "I replicate. It's what I do."
"You observed someone making white sauce?"
"I observed a cooking video on a laptop that still had battery power. I found it in the media lab."
"You watched a YouTube video?"
"I have discovered the internet. It's remarkable. Four billion years of isolation, and humans condensed all their knowledge into a system of talking rectangles."
Roxanne stared at him.
"I'm going to teach you about memes."
"I would like that."
On the fourth night, the wards broke.
Roxanne was asleep in the dean's office when Silas appeared in the doorway. His face was different — the shadows around him were agitated, flickering like candle flames in a draft.
"Get up."
She was awake instantly. "What?"
"Something is coming. Bigger than the surface-level entities. It felt the card. It's been tracking us for days."
She grabbed her jacket and the card. "How long?"
"Minutes. It's at the main gate."
From outside, a sound echoed through the campus. Not a scream. Not a crash. A bell — deep, resonant, wrong. It rang once, and the air vibrated with it.
"That's not a bell," Roxanne said.
"It's a summoning chime. It's announcing its presence. This one has manners."
"A polite monster?"
"A confident one. The ones that announce themselves are the ones that know they can't be stopped."
Roxanne ran for the gate. Silas followed. The Executioner was already there, chainsaw raised, standing between the main gate and the figure on the other side.

The figure was human-shaped. Tall. Wearing what looked like an antique business suit — pinstriped, double-breasted, from an era before anyone alive was born. Its face was smooth, featureless, like a mannequin's head covered in pale silk.
It held a briefcase.
"Ms. Vance," the figure said. Its voice was smooth, professional. "I represent the Nether-Grid Auditing Department. Your recent transactions have triggered a Tier-1 review."
"A review?"

"Yes. The acquisition of a Primordial artifact. The purchase of a Bound God-level entity. The territorial acquisition of a ley node campus. These are... unusual transactions for a newly awakened account."
The figure opened its briefcase. Inside was a single sheet of paper, glowing faintly.
"We need to verify your identity. Your bloodline. And your right to hold the card."
Roxanne looked at Silas.
"Is this normal?"
"There's never been a Primordial card activation in recorded history. There's no 'normal' for this situation."
"Wonderful."
She turned back to the suited figure.
"And if I refuse the audit?"
The figure closed its briefcase.
"Then the Department will escalate your case to the Enforcement Division. I don't recommend that. The Enforcement Division has significantly less patience than I do."
Behind the figure, the air rippled. Shapes moved in the darkness — things that were waiting. Watching.
Roxanne held up her card.
*DISPLAY: AUDITOR — NETHER-GRID COMPLIANCE DIVISION. PRICE: 2,000,000 OBOLS (BUYOUT).*
She smiled.
"Let me make you a counter-offer," she said. "I'll buy your contract. Right now. Two million Obols. You work for me instead."
The figure was silent for a long moment.
"That's... not standard procedure."
"Neither is an art student holding an infinite black card. We're both outside the box today."
The figure tilted its featureless head.
"I'll need to consult with my supervisor."
"You do that. Tell them the heir of the Primordial Void is open to negotiations. But the audit?"
Her smile sharpened.
"It's not happening."

*End of Chapter 10*
\n---\n
The auditor had nearly reached the main road when Roxanne caught up.
"Wait."
It stopped. Turned. Its featureless face regarded her with patience that felt practiced.
"Yes?"
"You came here to audit me. You brought Enforcement. You threatened to seize my card. And now you're walking away because I bought your team."
"That is an accurate summary."
"What happens next?"
"I will file a report. My supervisor will read it. A decision will be made at a higher level."
"And then?"
"And then either the matter will be dropped, or a higher-tier response will be dispatched."
"Higher-tier meaning?"."
"Praetorial level. The Council takes Primordial artifacts seriously."
Roxanne considered this. "If they send a Praetor, can I buy it too?"
The auditor paused. For a fraction of a second, its professional composure cracked.
"That is... not standard procedure."
"Neither is an art student holding a god's credit card. We're both outside the box today."
"I will mention your... purchasing enthusiasm... in my report."
"Please do. Tell them I'm open for business."
The auditor departed. Roxanne watched it go.
Silas appeared at her shoulder. "Was that wise?"
"Probably not. But it was fun."
"You enjoy antagonizing cosmic bureaucrats."
"I enjoy testing limits. The card has a limit somewhere — I need to know where."
"And if the limit turns out to be lower than a Praetor's price?"
"Then we improvise."
He sighed. A sound that carried four billion years of practice.
"You're not afraid of them," Silas said. "The three Primordials. You should be."
"I'm not afraid of much. It's a character flaw."
"It will get you killed."
"It hasn't yet."
She was lying, of course. She was terrified. But fear was a luxury she couldn't afford. The moment the Exchange sensed weakness, the prices would change. In the Nether-Grid, confidence was currency.
"Tell me about the Enforcement Division."
"What do you want to know?"
"Their hierarchy. Their budget. Their command structure. How many of them there are."
"You're planning to buy them."
"I'm planning to know their price. Buying is a separate decision."

Silas considered. "The Enforcement Division has approximately 200 active units. They're organized into five cohorts, each led by a Praetor. Above the Praetors is the Chief Arbiter, who reports directly to the First Seat."
"Two hundred units. At roughly 5 million each, that's... a billion Obols."
"You don't have a billion."
"I have infinity. A billion is just a number."
That evening, the campus held its first community dinner.

The dining hall was never designed for forty-seven people, but they made it work. Tables were pushed together. Someone found candles. The kitchen staff — two nursing students and a former restaurant chef who had been hiding in the engineering building — managed to produce a meal that was actual food, not just survival rations.
Roxanne sat at the head of the longest table, Silas beside her, Lena across from her. People ate. People talked. For a few hours, the apocalypse felt distant.
"This is what we're protecting," she said to Silas, gesturing at the room. "Not territory. Not power. This."
"I understand."
"Do you?"
"I spent four billion years alone. I understand the value of people being together more than most."
